5. Minimax Hypothesis Testing

5.1. Setup

Some applications are not well-matched to the Bayesian and Neyman-Pearson formulations. There may be a third scenario where we know costs but not priors. We adopt a zero-sum adversarial model where nature picks the worst prior for whatever rule we choose. This leads to robust but conservative design rules. Both players generally use a mixed strategy: nature with the prior and us with a randomized decision rule.

(5.1)

(5.2)

Note the ordering of the of the argmin and max: we goes first and nature goes second.

Example 5.1 (Intuition for Minimax). Let’s revisit Example 3.1

(5.4)

which is a linear function of for any such .

By optimality, the red curve is tangent to and lies above the blue curve. In this case the best case seems like choosing where the tangent is horizontal. The slope is zero when .

What if there is no such point, or the curve isn’t smooth, or we want to use something other than a deterministic LRT?

Intuition: There are two graphical representations: looking at the OC on the graph (below), and plotting on (Figure 13). In the latter, our geometric intuition suggests we need search a maximum on the graph, possibly at a kink. Equivalently, the condition for a horizontal line is when the slope is zero, which is given by an equalizer line on the graph:

(5.11)

If it intersects the OC curve we are done, otherwise we have to check the boundaries.

From Figure 13 it is clear that the adversary will always choose as the worst case prior. The conditional risks are

(5.12)

The adversary is choosing one of these. If , then so the adversary will always choose and we should minimize by setting (adversary always picks , under you never output 6). If its the other way around then and we want to set equal to 1 by choosing (adversary always picks , under you never output ).7 I think these two nonintersection cases correspond to monotonic functions.

Lemma 5.2. [TODO] The risk function exhibits at saddle point at the minmax.

The maximin version is just nature choosing the maximum Bayes risk and so that’s easy to interpret and consistent with our geometric intuition. Hence maximin is the same as minimax, and we have a Nash equilibrium (neither can improve). von Neumann showed that every zero sum game has a Nash.

Remarks:

  • Implication: when the likelihood ratio is purely continuous, the minimax decision rule is a likelihood rtaio and randomization is not required.
  • Guaranteed only one such intersection (in both geometric views) due to concavity in the vs graph and the fact that both curves are monotonic ( is decreasing line and the LRT is increasing)

  • The proof of the theorem establishes that the risk function exhibits a saddle point:

    (5.13)

    which further characterizes the minimax-optimal decision rule. In particular from the RHS we have

    Corollary 5.1. If is a minimax decision rule, then

    Moreover, if , then is a minimax decision rule.
